prompt: A ShiftWorks JobOps platform capable of running entirely offline provides employers, reviewers, and administrators a complete loop to create, approve, publish, and govern part-time job postings while maintaining strong internal controls. Employers use a Vue.js web console to draft job posts, preview how they will appear, and submit them for review with immediate, inline validation feedback (for example, hourly pay must be between $12.00 and $75.00, headcount must be 1–500, weekly hours must be 1–80, and a posting must include a US state and city selected from an admin-managed dictionary rather than any map). Each post supports structured pay and settlement choices (hourly vs. flat, weekly vs. end-of-shift), tags, and a validity period that defaults to 30 days and cannot exceed 90 days; employers can edit while in draft, unpublish active posts, and track status changes and reviewer notes. Reviewers work an approval queue with side-by-side comparisons for edits, can takedown posts for policy violations, and can process employer appeals with required rationale. Administrators operate an English-language admin console to manage users, job items, claims and report tickets, and dictionaries for categories and locations, and they view built-in dashboards showing post volume, claim success rate, average handling time, and reviewer activity. A self-service analytics and reporting center lets authorized users build dashboards by selecting configurable metrics and dimensions, schedule report generation (for example, daily at 2:00 AM server local time), and download masked exports; anomalies such as sudden spikes in takedowns or unusually low approval rates generate in-app alerts and appear in an admin alert inbox for follow-up. The backend uses Spring Boot to expose REST-style APIs consumed by the Vue.js frontend, enforcing decoupled validation, authorization, and persistence with MySQL as the local system of record. API-level RBAC governs every endpoint and object action (view/edit/approve/takedown/export), with rate controls such as 60 requests/minute per authenticated user and stricter limits on authentication attempts; brute-force protection includes a locally generated CAPTCHA after 3 failed logins and an account lock for 15 minutes after 5 failures. Passwords must be at least 12 characters with complexity rules, are salted and hashed (bcrypt), and must be rotated every 90 days; critical actions such as publishing, takedown, role changes, and exporting unmasked data require step-up verification by re-entering the current password. Session management supports idle timeout (30 minutes), absolute timeout (12 hours), and single logout that invalidates all active sessions for the user. Input protection includes strict parameter validation, prepared statements, output encoding to reduce XSS risk, and CSRF tokens for state-changing requests; all key operations write immutable audit trails (who, what, when, before/after) to MySQL. Sensitive fields (for example, contact phone numbers stored for job coordination) are encrypted at rest with AES-256 at the column level and masked on screen and in exports by default, showing only the last 4 digits unless a privileged role is granted. File uploads (attachments for claims, appeals, and tickets) are stored on the local server file system with per-record access checks, a whitelist of PDF/JPG/PNG, a 10 MB size limit, basic file signature verification and checksum fingerprinting, and mandatory watermarks on exported PDFs; any file that fails validation is quarantined for admin review. Backup and restore are offline-first, with nightly encrypted backups retained for 30 days and a tested restore workflow limited to administrators.
